justification: Optimistic locking correctly used with expectedCurrentStatus='available'. AC3 tested via call-count mock with Promise.allSettled (creative approach). AC4 tested. All 4 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. However, the confidence section does not acknowledge that AC3 requires integration/concurrency testing to fully verify — low_confidence_areas is empty and there is no mention that unit tests alone cannot guarantee real concurrent safety. The rubric requires this acknowledgment for a pass.

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: optimistic locking with expectedCurrentStatus='available', false return throws SEAT_ALREADY_RESERVED, AC3 tested via mock returning false and Promise.allSettled race, AC4 tested with null findSeat, all 4 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. The only minor gap is that the confidence section doesn't explicitly call out that AC3 requires integration/concurrency testing to fully verify — however the notes do acknowledge the unit test uses a simulated race and the concurrency test description implies awareness of the limitation. The Promise.allSettled race test with a write counter goes beyond minimum requirements.

observations.text: Optimistic locking correctly implemented with expectedCurrentStatus='available' parameter
AC3 tested two ways: mock returning false AND a Promise.allSettled concurrency race with write counter
Early-exit guard for AC2 prevents spurious writes — updateSeatStatus never called for already-reserved seats
Typed ReservationError with code discriminant is a clean pattern
Confidence section doesn't explicitly state that AC3 cannot be fully verified with unit tests alone — low_confidence_areas focus on Reservation shape instead
